caption: Koilonychia may result from faulty iron metabolism and is one of the signs of Plummer-Vinson syndrome, as well as of hemochromatosis. Spoon nails have been observed in coronary disease, syphilis, polycythemia, and acanthosis nigricans. Familial forms are also known to occur. Other associations include psoriasis, lichen planus, Raynaud's disease, scleroderma, acromegaly, hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism, monilethrix, palmar hyperkeratoses, and steatocystoma multiplex. A significant number of cases are idiopathic. Manual trauma in combination with cold exposure may result in seasonal disease. Sherpas, Tibetan people living in the Nepalese Himalayas who often serve as porters on mountain-climbing expeditions, frequently exhibit koilonychia due to chronic cold exposure and hypoxemia.

caption:
Impetiginization is the term for impetigo imposed upon preexisting dermatoses, most commonly insect bites and atopic dermatitis. Eruptions that are pruritic are particularly susceptible to secondary infection. The most common organisms are streptococci and staphylococci.
Ecthyma occurs when there is ulceration beneath the surface of a skin infection. If impetigo is an infection by streptococci and/or staphylococci superficially in the epidermis, ecthyma is an infection by the same organisms through the entire thickness of the epidermis (0.1 mm) to the upper reaches of the dermis (perhaps to a depth of 0.5 mm). Clinically, there is often a firm crust covering a superficial ulcer, surrounded by erythema.

caption: The patient, a 23-year-old male, reported developing rashes after contact with water for over a year. The typical skin lesions manifest as translucent white or yellow edematous papules, plaques with excessive whitish keratinization, sometimes with dilated sweat gland pores and palmar hyperhidrosis. Symptoms can include pain, burning sensations, and itching, though sometimes there are no symptoms. No discomfort was reported in this specific case. Aquagenic keratoderma discussed in the post is a rare keratinization disorder, more commonly seen in females. It is characterized by skin lesions that appear after brief immersion in hot or cold water and disappear within 30-60 minutes after drying. The pathology is noted by excessive keratinization, with thickening of the granular and spinous layers and elongation of epithelial crests. Possible causes include genetic factors and abnormal protein causing disruption in the skin’s water barrier. Differential diagnoses include aquagenic urticaria, aquagenic pruritus, aquadynia, and tinea manuum. There is no specific treatment, but relief may be provided by topical use of 20% salicylic acid ointment, 10% urea ointment, and aluminum chloride. Avoiding water and irritants like soap is recommended.

caption: Livedo reticularis is a netlike mottled or reticulated pink or reddish-blue discoloration of the skin, mostly on the extremities, especially the legs. Exposure to cold usually accentuates the intensity of the discoloration, although the lesions may be fixed and remain present on warming, sometimes referred to as livedo racemosa. The lesions are often asymptomatic; in other cases, symptoms such as coldness, numbness, paresthesia, or a dull ache may be present. Necrotizing livedo reticularis may be associated with cutaneous nodules and ulcerations. Most cases are unassociated with any underlying cause, but livedo reticularis may be a manifestation of lupus erythematosus, dermatomyositis, scleroderma, rheumatic fever, or rheumatoid arthritis. It may also be seen with hepatitis C, parvovirus B19, syphilis, meningococcemia, pneumococcal sepsis, tuberculosis, pancreatitis, decompression sickness, various forms of arteritis, Wegener granulomatosis, polycythemia vera, hypercalcemia, pheochromocytoma, mycosis fungoides, breast cancer, and thrombocytopenic purpura. It is a side effect of amantadine, and has been reported as a photosensitivity phenomenon in patients taking quinidine. Some patients on long-term minocycline therapy have developed drug-induced arthritis, fever, livedo reticularis, and positive peripheral antineutrophil cytoplasmic autoantibodies levels. Livedo reticularis may be a complication of arteriography.

caption: A 40-year-old male presents with scattered, soybean-sized, spot-like white patches on his chest that have been present for 2 years. These patches are asymptomatic, unchanged by treatment for sweat spots, do not worsen in summer or lighten in winter, and show no signs of enlargement. The consensus among forum users supports the diagnosis of idiopathic guttate hypomelanosis. Features discussed include typically circular or oval patches, less than 1cm in diameter, with a color transitioning to porcelain white, and a clear boundary without merging or expansion, though the number of spots may increase with age. Predominantly found on the trunk and limbs, these patches can slightly depress over time and are considered by some as a sign of skin aging. Differential diagnoses include tinea versicolor and various other conditions presenting with white patches, but the unique characteristics of non-merging, non-scaling patches differentiate idiopathic guttate hypomelanosis. No cure was discussed, and treatment remains unclear.

caption: Neurofibromatosis Type I: These are illustrations of the pigmentary anomalies and tumors in the skin of patients with neurofibromatosis. Several café-au-lait spots and small tumors are shown. A large café-au-lait spot on the left and a tumor on the right are also depicted. Neurofibromas have variable consistency. The soft lesions can be pushed into the surrounding skin, a process called "buttonholing." Neurofibromas can cause cosmetic disfigurement. Pruritus of the skin that overlies the neurofibroma is a common complaint, and the itching is aggravated by exertion or a warm environment. Malignant degeneration of a benign neurofibroma can occur. In addition, patients with the disease are prone to developing neurofibrosarcomas or malignant schwannomas de novo. Selective surgical removal of neurofibromas, in order to improve either appearance or function, is feasible.

caption: Conradi-Hünermann syndrome is a rare genetic disorder with multiple systemic effects. Affected infants have generalized redness and scaling (ichthyosiform erythroderma). The lesions occur in a whorled or linear, blotchy pattern. Older children and young adults develop follicular atrophoderma and sometimes dark and light patches of skin and patchy scarring alopecia. Conradi-Hünermann syndrome is a form of chondrodysplasia punctata, a group of genetic disorders with shortening of bones and stippled epiphyses. This disorder is also associated with cataracts, which may be present at birth or develop later, short stature, and a characteristic facial appearance. It is caused by mutations of the emopamil-binding protein (EBP) gene and is inherited as an X-linked dominant trait, occurring almost exclusively in girls.

caption: This image discusses Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome, also known as hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, which is diagnosed using the Curaçao criteria. Imaging is used to detect these malformations, and a diagnosis of Osler-Weber-Rendu syndrome can be made if the patient fulfills three criteria. The syndrome may present with epistaxis and telangiectasia on the face.

Other differential diagnoses discussed include eczema or drug rash.

caption: This image shows various skin diseases, including patch, plaque, and nodular stages of Kaposi's sarcoma, which are characterized by different appearances and causes. The patch stage features angiogenesis and increased density of inflammatory cells, while the plaque stage has large hyaline deposits and autofluorescence. The nodular stage involves spindle cells and blood vessels, and can progress to solid nodules with the presence of the HIV virus. These diseases can cause symptoms such as skin lesions, muscle effusions, and lymph node enlargement, and can be treated with chemotherapy and surgery.

caption: Female, 66 years old, experienced subcutaneous bleeding (purpura) in both upper limbs starting one week ago, symmetrically. There is no history of medication prior to the incident, allergic to sulfa drugs, and coagulation tests were normal. No specific medication or cause identified from the blood tests. The discussed symptoms and observations suggest a diagnosis of senile purpura. Senile purpura typically presents as well-demarcated yet irregularly shaped violet patches, mainly occurring on the extensor surfaces, forearms, and backs of the hands in elderly individuals, resulting from fragile blood vessels due to weakened collagen support, where minor trauma can cause vessel rupture and lead to purpura. Users recommended no specific treatment as it generally does not require intervention but suggested long-term use of Danshen tablets and Vitamin C supplements. Additionally, internal use of Vitamin K and Vitamin C was mentioned as a treatment.

The primary condition discussed among forum members is progressive finger palm keratoderma, which is characterized by dry, scaly, and cracking skin on fingers and palms, typically worsening in cold seasons and potentially exacerbated by soap and solvent exposure. A compound called rapamycin, used to prevent kidney rejection, may be effective in treating angiofibroma, and has recently received FDA approval for this purpose.
